---
title: "Claude Code for Clinical Programming"
subtitle: "Reference Implementation Guide"
author: "Brian Carter"
date: last-modified
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
---

## Setup

### Prerequisites

Before using this repo, you need Claude Code installed and configured in Positron. Follow the setup guide:

- [Claude Code Configuration for Positron](https://connect.posit.npowermedicine.com/claude-code-config/)

The RAG server for CDISC standards lookup also needs to be configured separately:

- [MCP RAG Server Setup](https://connect.posit.npowermedicine.com/claude-mcp-rag/)

### Clone the Reference Repo

```bash
git clone https://github.com/N-Power-Medicine/claude-analytics-ref.git
cd claude-analytics-ref
```

Once cloned, open the project folder in Positron. The `.vscode/tasks.json` will auto-launch Claude Code and a local shell. All rules, skills, commands, and agents are loaded automatically --- no additional configuration needed.

To initialize the R environment:

```r
renv::restore()
```

This installs all approved packages from `renv.lock`.


## What This Repo Is

This repository is a **reference implementation** demonstrating how to configure Claude Code for clinical programming workflows in R. It shows how four configuration layers --- rules, skills, commands, and agents --- compose together to enforce standards, automate workflows, and orchestrate multi-step tasks.

The goal is to give your team a working example they can clone, customize, and extend for their own projects.

### What You Get Out of the Box

| Capability | How it works |
|------------|-------------|
| Enforced coding standards | 8 rule files covering R style, approved packages, CDISC conventions, data safety, and more |
| Automated R code workflow | Write → test → execute → validate, every time |
| Databricks integration patterns | Connection templates, schema navigation, performance best practices |
| CDISC standards lookup | RAG-powered search over SDTM-IG and controlled terminology |
| Multi-agent pipeline | Planner → Programmer → Reviewer, mirroring clinical QC |
| Project scaffolding | One command to set up a new R project with renv and standard structure |
| Team onboarding | Interactive walkthrough for new members |


## Architecture

Claude Code loads configuration in layers. Every conversation gets the full stack --- you never need to manually activate rules or remind Claude about standards.

```
Every conversation loads:
  CLAUDE.md          → orientation (what is this project?)
  + all rules/       → constraints (what standards apply?)
  + relevant skill   → workflow (how do I produce the output?)
  + command if invoked → action (run this specific task)
  + agent if spawned  → role (who does this work?)
```

### How the Pieces Fit Together

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 9
%%| fig-cap: "Claude Code configuration architecture"
flowchart TB
    subgraph Always["Always Loaded (every conversation)"]
        CM["CLAUDE.md<br/><i>Project orientation</i>"]
        subgraph Rules[".claude/rules/"]
            R1["r-style.md"]
            R2["approved-packages.md"]
            R3["namespace-conflicts.md"]
            R4["cdisc-conventions.md"]
            R5["file-layout.md"]
            R6["data-safety.md"]
            R7["git-conventions.md"]
            R8["error-messages.md"]
        end
    end

    subgraph AutoFire["Auto-Invoked Skills"]
        S1["r-code<br/><i>Write → Test → Execute</i>"]
        S2["databricks<br/><i>Connection & query patterns</i>"]
    end

    subgraph OnDemand["User-Invoked Commands"]
        C1["/r-project<br/><i>Scaffold new project</i>"]
        C2["/ct-lookup<br/><i>CDISC terminology</i>"]
        C3["/onboard<br/><i>Team walkthrough</i>"]
    end

    subgraph Agents["Spawned Agents"]
        A1["feature-planner<br/><i>Opus · Architect</i>"]
        A2["r-clinical-programmer<br/><i>Sonnet · Implementer</i>"]
        A3["code-reviewer<br/><i>Sonnet · QC Verifier</i>"]
    end

    subgraph External["External Services"]
        MCP["CDISC RAG Server<br/><i>npm-rag-v1</i>"]
        DB["Databricks<br/><i>via con / sc</i>"]
    end

    CM --> Rules
    Rules --> AutoFire
    Rules --> OnDemand
    Rules --> Agents
    C2 --> MCP
    S2 --> DB
    A1 -->|"plan"| A2
    A2 -->|"implement"| A3
    A3 -->|"QC report"| A3

    style Always fill:#f0f4ff,stroke:#4a6fa5
    style Rules fill:#e8eef7,stroke:#4a6fa5
    style AutoFire fill:#f0fff0,stroke:#2d8a4e
    style OnDemand fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#c79100
    style Agents fill:#fef0f0,stroke:#c94040
    style External fill:#f5f0ff,stroke:#7b5ea7
```

### The Standard Agent Pipeline

For complex features, the three agents work in sequence --- mirroring the clinical programming QC model where one programmer writes and an independent reviewer verifies.

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-cap: "Agent orchestration pipeline"
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant FP as feature-planner<br/>(Opus)
    participant RCP as r-clinical-programmer<br/>(Sonnet)
    participant CR as code-reviewer<br/>(Sonnet)

    U->>FP: "Build an AE simulation program"
    FP->>U: Clarifying questions
    U->>FP: Answers
    FP->>FP: Assess codebase, identify risks
    FP-->>RCP: plans/plan_sim_ae_2026-03-26.md

    RCP->>RCP: Read plan
    RCP->>RCP: Write programs/sim_ae.R
    RCP->>RCP: Write tests/test-sim_ae.R
    RCP->>RCP: Execute & fix until clean
    RCP-->>CR: Implementation complete

    CR->>CR: Read plan + implementation
    CR->>CR: Run tests
    CR->>CR: Check rules & CDISC compliance
    CR-->>U: QC Report (PASS / FAIL)
```


## Configuration Layers

### Rules (`.claude/rules/`)

**Always-on project constraints.** Every rule file is loaded into context for every conversation, regardless of the task. Rules define *what standards this project enforces*.

| Rule | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `r-style.md` | Tidyverse style: `snake_case`, `%>%` pipe, section headers, 80-char lines |
| `approved-packages.md` | Whitelist of allowed packages --- Claude asks before using anything else |
| `namespace-conflicts.md` | Known conflicts (huxtable/pharmaRTF, dplyr/stats) and resolution patterns |
| `cdisc-conventions.md` | USUBJID format, ISO 8601 dates, study day formula, cross-domain consistency |
| `file-layout.md` | Directory structure (`R/`, `tests/`, `programs/`, `data/`, `output/`) and naming |
| `data-safety.md` | No hardcoded credentials, no real patient data, `set.seed()` for simulations |
| `git-conventions.md` | Branch naming (`sdtm/`, `tfl/`, `feat/`), commit format, PR requirements |
| `error-messages.md` | Standard patterns for `stop()`, `warning()`, `message()` with `call. = FALSE` |

Rules are *policies* --- they say what must be true, not how to do it.

### Skills (`.claude/skills/`)

**Procedural workflows that auto-invoke.** Skills define *how* work gets done. They fire automatically when the task matches.

#### `r-code` --- R Code Generation

Triggers on any R code request. Enforces a **3-artifact workflow**:

1. **Write** the function file (`R/<name>.R`) with roxygen2 documentation
2. **Write** the test file (`tests/test-<name>.R`) with testthat
3. **Execute** both and iterate until clean --- no code is delivered until it runs

This means every function Claude produces comes with tests and a confirmation that the code actually works.

#### `databricks` --- Databricks Integration

Triggers when you ask about connecting to Databricks, querying tables, or pulling data. Covers:

- **Two connection objects**: `con` (DBI/ODBC for data retrieval) and `sc` (sparklyr for Spark-native operations)
- **Three-part namespace**: `<catalog>.<schema>.<table>` with `in_catalog()` for dplyr
- **Performance patterns**: `collect()` (pull to R), `compute()` (temp Spark table), `cache()` (pin in memory)
- **Anti-patterns** to avoid (e.g., collecting before filtering)

#### `ads-data` --- ADS Data Access

Triggers when pulling, loading, exploring, or subsetting ADS (Analytical Dataset) data from Databricks. Covers:

- **`get_ads()` function**: Pull ADS data by cohort (`"lung"`, `"bladder"`, `"breast"`, `"ovarian"`) and type (`"enriched"` vs `"essentials"`)
- **Enriched vs Essentials**: Enriched = manually abstracted, highest quality, full variable coverage. Essentials = broader population, structured sources only.
- **Nested JSON columns**: Systemic therapy, radiation, biomarkers, and line-of-therapy stored as JSON strings requiring `parse_json_col()` to expand
- **Critical rule**: Always deduplicate to one row per `patientid` before parsing JSON to avoid explosive row duplication
- **Patient identifier**: Always use `patientid` (UUID), count with `n_distinct(patientid)`

### Commands (`.claude/commands/`)

**User-invoked actions.** Commands run only when you type `/command-name`.

| Command | What it does |
|---------|-------------|
| `/r-project` | Scaffolds a new R project: creates `R/`, `tests/`, `data/` dirs, initializes renv with all approved packages, generates `.Rprofile` with Databricks connections, creates `main.R` template |
| `/ct-lookup` | Queries the CDISC RAG server for controlled terminology. Example: `/ct-lookup VSTESTCD` returns a formatted table of valid values |
| `/onboard` | Interactive 5-step walkthrough for new team members: orient, demonstrate capabilities, explain rules, list commands, wrap up |

### Agents (`.claude/agents/`)

**Specialized subprocesses for complex work.** Each agent has its own model, role, and persistent memory.

#### `feature-planner` (Opus)

The architect. Runs *before* any coding begins.

- **Discovery**: Reads the codebase, reviews existing patterns and dependencies
- **Assessment**: Asks 3--7 clarifying questions, pushes back on problems, suggests enhancements
- **Planning**: Produces a structured markdown plan in `plans/plan_<feature>_<date>.md`
- **Orchestration**: Ends every plan with a task table assigning work to other agents

#### `r-clinical-programmer` (Sonnet)

The implementer. Follows plans and writes all R code.

- **Mandatory execution**: Every line of code is run before it's returned --- no exceptions
- **Iterate on errors**: If code fails, diagnoses and fixes automatically until clean
- **Plan-aware**: Reads the plan from `plans/` and follows the orchestration guide
- **Deep expertise**: Tidyverse, admiral, xportr, pharmaverse, CDISC standards

#### `code-reviewer` (Sonnet)

The independent QC gate. Reviews but does **not** write production code.

- **Systematic checklist**: Plan alignment, rule compliance, code quality, test coverage, CDISC compliance
- **Severity levels**: BLOCKING (must fix), WARNING (should fix), NOTE (suggestions)
- **Runs tests**: Executes the test suite and reports results
- **Structured report**: Produces a QC report with a verdict: PASS / PASS WITH WARNINGS / FAIL


## Clinical Programming RAG Server

The project connects to a local RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) server via MCP (Model Context Protocol). This gives Claude access to the full SDTM Implementation Guide, controlled terminology codelists, and any other documents you ingest.

The server is configured in `.mcp.json` at the project root and runs as a local Python process backed by an SQLite vector store. It uses hybrid search (keyword + semantic) to return scored results from ingested documents.

### How It's Used

The `/ct-lookup` command is the primary user-facing interface to the RAG. When you run `/ct-lookup VSTESTCD`, Claude queries the vector store and formats the results as a clean table of valid codes and decodes.

Agents also use the RAG directly --- the `code-reviewer` verifies controlled terminology values in generated code, and the `r-clinical-programmer` looks up variable specifications while implementing SDTM mappings.

::: {.callout-note}
For server setup, document ingestion, and index management, see the [MCP RAG Server documentation](https://connect.posit.npowermedicine.com/claude-mcp-rag/).
:::


## Prompt Guide

How you phrase your request determines which parts of the system activate. Here are patterns that work well.

### Simple R Code

Just ask. The `r-code` skill fires automatically.

> "Write a function that calculates study days from a reference date and an event date"

> "Write a function that formats p-values for a TFL"

**What happens:** Claude writes the function in `R/`, writes tests in `tests/`, executes both, and reports results. You get working, tested code.

### Databricks Queries

Mention Databricks, tables, or data pulls. The `databricks` skill fires automatically.

> "Pull the active treatment lines from the lung lot table"

> "Show me the schema for development.ads.enriched_lung_lot"

> "How many rows are in the patients table?"

### CDISC Lookups

Ask plain-English questions about CDISC standards. Claude queries the RAG server automatically.

> "What are the acceptable values for RACE in CDISC?"

> "What vital signs test codes are valid for VSTESTCD?"

> "What are the controlled terminology options for adverse event severity?"

> "How many ADS datasets do we have and what is the difference between enriched and essentials?"

### Planning a Feature

Describe what you want to build. Claude will engage the `feature-planner` agent.

> "I want to build a simulation program for the AE domain"

> "Plan a TFL program for Table 14.1.1 --- demographics summary"

> "I need to refactor the TFL pipeline to support multiple output formats"

> "I need the feature planner to scope out an R program. It needs to pull from the lung ADS dataset and subset only to cases diagnosed in 2025. At the end of the program, provide a frequency table of histological subtypes diagnosed across months of diagnosis."

**What happens:** The planner asks clarifying questions, reviews the codebase, and produces a plan in `plans/`. Then you can tell Claude to implement it.

### Running the Full Pipeline

After planning, tell Claude to implement and review.

> "Implement the plan in plans/plan_sim_ae_2026-03-26.md"

> "Now run the code reviewer on the AE simulation"

### Quick Questions

Ask anything about project standards --- Claude reads the rules.

> "What packages are approved for this project?"

> "How should I name my branch for a new SDTM domain?"

> "What's the study day calculation formula?"

### Scaffolding a New Project

> `/r-project`

This creates the full directory structure, initializes renv, installs all approved packages, and generates `.Rprofile` and `main.R`.


## How to Create New Configuration

### Creating a New Rule

Rules go in `.claude/rules/` as markdown files. They are always loaded, so keep them focused and concise.

**When to create a rule:** When a constraint applies across *all* tasks --- code generation, review, refactoring, testing.

**Template:**

```markdown
# Rule Title

Brief description of what this rule enforces and why.

## Section 1

- Specific requirement
- Specific requirement

## Section 2

| Pattern | Example |
|---------|---------|
| what to do | how it looks |

## Exceptions

- When this rule does NOT apply
```

**Guidelines:**

- One concern per rule file (don't combine style and safety in one file)
- State what must be true, not how to achieve it (that's what skills are for)
- Include examples of correct and incorrect patterns
- Reference other rules by filename when there's overlap
- Add the rule to the table in `CLAUDE.md` so the team knows it exists

### Creating a New Skill

Skills go in `.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`. They auto-invoke based on their description.

**When to create a skill:** When a specific task type needs a repeatable, multi-step workflow.

**Template:**

```markdown
---
name: my-skill
description: Auto-invoked when [trigger condition]. Governs [what workflow].
---

# Skill Title

Brief description of what this skill does and when it fires.

## Core Principle

The single most important rule for this workflow.

## Workflow

### Step 1: [Action]
What to do first.

### Step 2: [Action]
What to do next.

### Step 3: [Validation]
How to confirm the output is correct.

## Templates

Code or file templates that should be followed.

## Edge Cases

How to handle situations where the normal workflow doesn't apply.
```

**Guidelines:**

- The `description` field in the frontmatter controls when the skill fires --- make it specific
- Don't restate rules (they're inherited automatically). Reference them by name.
- Focus on *procedure* (how), not *policy* (what) --- policy belongs in rules
- Include templates for any files the skill generates
- Define what "done" looks like (validation criteria)

### Creating a New Command

Commands go in `.claude/commands/<command-name>.md`. They run only when the user types `/command-name`.

**When to create a command:** For on-demand, one-shot tasks like scaffolding, generation, or lookup.

**Template:**

```markdown
---
description: One-line description shown in the command list
---

# Command Title

Describe the user's intent when invoking this command.

## What to do

1. Step-by-step instructions for Claude to follow
2. Include specifics about tools to use, output format, etc.

## Output Format

How the result should be presented to the user.

## Error Handling

What to do when things go wrong.
```

**Guidelines:**

- The `description` field shows up in `/help` output --- keep it under 80 characters
- Commands should be self-contained (the user shouldn't need to provide additional context unless the command asks for it)
- Include the expected output format so results are consistent

### Creating a New Agent

Agents go in `.claude/agents/<agent-name>.md`. They are spawned by the main conversation or by other agents.

**When to create an agent:** When a role requires a distinct persona, model choice, or tool scope.

**Template:**

```markdown
---
name: my-agent
description: "When to spawn this agent and example triggers.

Examples:

- User: \"example request\"
  Assistant: \"I'll use the my-agent to handle this.\"
  [Uses Agent tool to launch my-agent]"
model: sonnet  # or opus for planning/architecture tasks
color: blue    # terminal color: red, green, blue, yellow, cyan, magenta
memory: project  # or agent
---

You are a [role description]. Your expertise includes [domains].

## Core Behavioral Rules

1. **Rule 1.** What this agent must always do.
2. **Rule 2.** What this agent must never do.

## Workflow

### Step 1: [Phase]
What to do and how.

### Step 2: [Phase]
What to do and how.

## Output Format

The exact structure of what this agent returns.

## Communication Style

How this agent should communicate (direct, friendly, formal, etc.).

## Update your agent memory
[Instructions for what to record in persistent memory]
```

**Guidelines:**

- The `description` field tells the main conversation *when* to spawn this agent --- include example triggers
- Choose `model: opus` for planning and architecture, `model: sonnet` for implementation and review
- The `memory` field sets memory scope: `project` (shared across agents) or `agent` (private)
- Don't restate rules or skills --- agents inherit everything automatically
- Define a clear output format so results are predictable
- Include memory instructions so the agent builds institutional knowledge over time

### Creating a New Plan

Plans are generated by the `feature-planner` agent, but you can also create them manually. They go in `plans/plan_<feature>_<date>.md`.

**Structure:**

```markdown
# Implementation Plan: [Feature Name]
**Date:** [YYYY-MM-DD]
**Status:** Draft | Approved

## 1. Overview
What the feature does and why.

## 2. Requirements
- Functional requirements
- Non-functional requirements
- Clarifications from discussion

## 3. Current State Assessment
- Relevant existing code
- Dependencies
- Known constraints

## 4. Proposed Design
- Architecture and approach
- Key functions to create or modify
- Data flow

## 5. Enhancements
- Enhancement 1: description and rationale
- Enhancement 2: description and rationale

## 6. Risks and Mitigations
- Risk → Mitigation

## 7. Testing Strategy
- Unit tests
- Integration tests
- Validation approach

## 8. Orchestration Guide

| Task | Agent | Priority | Dependencies | Description |
|------|-------|----------|--------------|-------------|
| 1 | r-clinical-programmer | P1 | None | [task] |
| 2 | code-reviewer | P2 | Task 1 | [task] |
```

The orchestration guide at the end is what makes plans actionable --- it tells Claude exactly which agent handles each task and in what order.


## File Layout

```
.claude/
  rules/              ← Always-on constraints (8 rule files)
  skills/             ← Auto-invoked workflows (r-code, databricks)
  commands/           ← User-invoked actions (/r-project, /ct-lookup, /onboard)
  agents/             ← Specialized roles (planner, programmer, reviewer)
  agent-memory/       ← Persistent memory for each agent
R/                    ← Reusable function files (one function per file)
tests/                ← testthat test files (test-<name>.R)
programs/             ← Analysis scripts, simulations, data pulls, mappings
data/                 ← SDTM/ADaM XPT datasets
  raw/                ← Raw source data from Databricks
output/               ← RTF tables, figures, listings
plans/                ← Implementation plans from feature-planner
docs/                 ← Documentation and specs
artifacts/            ← Study documents (SAP, data dictionaries, table shells)
example-code/         ← Reference examples (Databricks connection patterns)
```


## Quick Reference

| I want to... | Do this |
|--------------|---------|
| Write an R function | Just describe it --- `r-code` skill handles the rest |
| Look up CDISC terminology | `/ct-lookup <variable>` |
| Start a new project | `/r-project` |
| Plan a complex feature | Describe what you want to build |
| Run QC on code | "Review the [file] I just wrote" |
| Query Databricks | Mention the table or schema you need |
| Onboard a new team member | `/onboard` |
| Check project standards | Ask any question about rules |
